entry.title: Polyfill, npm Worms, Salesforce Exposure, and CVE-2026-28318 Exploitation
entry.content:
This coverage window was dominated by reports of trusted infrastructure abuse across web dependencies, developer ecosystems, cloud identity flows, and SaaS platforms. Several items describe attackers using legitimate services, valid credentials, or misconfigurations to create convincing phishing paths, steal data, or maintain persistence while blending into expected operational patterns. Toshiba and Muji warned visitors after suspicious sign-in prompts appeared on their websites via scripts loaded from polyfill.io. Security researcher Pasquale Pillitteri reported that polyfill.io became active again in late May 2026 and began returning HTTP 401 authentication requests, causing browser login prompts; Japanese media also reported impacts at Zojirushi, FiNC Technologies, Ishiyaku Publishers, and Hobonichi. Multiple reports described continued pressure on developer ecosystems. In one supply chain scenario, TeamPCP exploited a misconfigured GitHub Actions pullrequesttarget workflow in Aqua Security’s Trivy repository, obtained an aqua-bot PAT, and force-pushed release tags in aquasecurity/trivy-action to malicious commits that harvested secrets before running the legitimate scanner. Separate npm campaigns distributed the Rust-based IronWorm stealer and a new Miasma worm variant through poisoned packages, compromised npm accounts, preinstall execution, and a Phantom Gyp technique using binding.gyp. Censys highlighted dangling DNS records as another form of trusted infrastructure exposure. Stale CNAME, NS, and third-party service records can allow attackers to claim abandoned resources and operate phishing pages, malware delivery, fake login portals, redirects, or defacement content under an organization’s legitimate subdomain. Mitiga described an AWS CLI phishing method abusing the aws login --remote flow, where attackers generate a legitimate cross-device authentication URL, lure victims through real AWS sign-in pages, and complete the attacker’s CLI login using the verification code. The proof-of-concept used AWS-hosted infrastructure including S3, DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway, EC2, and SSM, placing much of the phishing infrastructure on amazonaws.com domains. A separate Mitiga report attributed Salesforce data exposure to ShinyHunters’ abuse of misconfigured Experience Cloud Guest User Profile permissions rather than a compromise of Salesforce itself. The activity reportedly involved a modified version of Mandiant’s AuraInspector tool to scan Salesforce sites and extract data through the /s/sfsites/aura endpoint, with ShinyHunters claiming access to data from 300 to 400 organizations. Mitiga also published broader analysis on cloud detection and alerting models. The articles argued that attacks increasingly span Okta, Salesforce, AWS, Kubernetes, S3, workloads, and data services as a single attack path, while static correlation can miss credential misuse and living-off-the-land behavior when attackers vary timing, sequence, or methodology. CISA added CVE-2026-28318, a high-severity SolarWinds Serv-U denial-of-service flaw, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog after warning of active exploitation. SolarWinds said the issue is caused by uncontrolled resource consumption triggered by crafted unauthenticated POST requests using Content-Encoding: deflate, and released Serv-U 15.5.4 Hotfix 1. BleepingComputer reported on Volexity findings that Chinese espionage group UNC5221, also tracked as VerdantBamboo, used Brickstorm and newly documented malware named Plenet and AgentPSD to access Microsoft 365 environments and maintain access. Volexity found the actor had been present in one victim network for at least 18 months, had compromised the victim’s managed services provider, and later re-entered using stolen credentials to configure SSL VPN access and deploy malware to a Synology NAS device. Netskope summarized a June 2, 2026 Executive Order directing U.S. federal agencies to accelerate AI adoption while strengthening cybersecurity and national security protections. The order calls for Binding Operational Directives through CISA and OMB consultation, an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse, and classified benchmarking of advanced cyber capabilities in AI models. Health-ISAC published its Health Care and Social Assistance Threat Landscape Report on June 5, covering January 1 through March 31, 2026. Produced by ReliaQuest, the report focuses on ransomware and data-leak activity, vulnerability exploitation, attacker targeting, top techniques, data risk protection, and countries targeted in the health care and social assistance sector.

ShinyHunters: Salesforce’s Uninvited Guest User Profile Breach | Mitiga — Must Read

ShinyHunters reportedly exploited misconfigured Salesforce Experience Cloud Guest User permissions, not a Salesforce vulnerability, to access CRM data through the public Aura endpoint at scale. Organizations using Salesforce Experience Cloud should immediately audit guest access, disable guest API permissions, and monitor /s/sfsites/aura traffic for bulk unauthenticated queries.

  • The article says ShinyHunters did not hack Salesforce itself but abused misconfigured Salesforce Experience Cloud Guest User Profile permissions that exposed data to unauthenticated users.
  • ShinyHunters reportedly modified Mandiant’s open-source AuraInspector tool to scan Salesforce sites, identify overly permissive guest profiles, and extract data through the /s/sfsites/aura endpoint at scale.
  • The article states that unauthenticated attackers can query internal CRM records when guest user profiles have API access enabled and permissions to sensitive objects such as Contacts or Accounts.
  • ShinyHunters claims to have breached 300 to 400 organizations and extracted CRM records including customer names, phone numbers, financial data, and business relationships.
  • The article recommends disabling API access for guest users, reviewing guest object and field permissions, enabling secure guest user record access, and monitoring /s/sfsites/aura traffic for high-volume unauthenticated queries.

The Domino Effect of a Software Supply Chain Attack — Must Read

A misconfigured GitHub Actions pull_request_target workflow let TeamPCP steal credentials and rapidly compromise multiple software supply chain ecosystems, including GitHub, Docker Hub, npm, Open VSX, and PyPI. The key lesson is that mutable tags, over-permissioned CI/CD secrets, and weak cross-platform correlation can turn one exposed token into a broad cascading compromise.

  • TeamPCP began the campaign by exploiting a misconfigured GitHub Actions pull_request_target workflow in Aqua Security’s Trivy repository, exposing the aqua-bot service account Personal Access Token.
  • Using the stolen PAT, TeamPCP force-pushed nearly all release tags in aquasecurity/trivy-action to malicious commits, causing pipelines that referenced those tags to pull attacker-controlled code.
  • The malicious Trivy payload searched process memory and filesystems for secrets, encrypted the stolen data, exfiltrated it to a typosquatted domain, and then ran the legitimate Trivy scanner so workflows appeared normal.
  • Credentials harvested from compromised environments enabled TeamPCP to spread into npm, the Open VSX Registry, Checkmarx KICS GitHub Action tags, and PyPI through malicious packages and extensions.
  • The article emphasizes that effective detection requires correlating identity behavior across GitHub, container registries, package managers, and cloud infrastructure rather than monitoring each platform in isolation.

CISA: Hackers now exploit SolarWinds Serv-U flaw to crash servers — Must Read

CISA says attackers are actively exploiting a newly patched SolarWinds Serv-U denial-of-service flaw, CVE-2026-28318, that can crash servers via unauthenticated crafted POST requests. Organizations should install Serv-U 15.5.4 Hotfix 1 or apply SolarWinds’ mitigations immediately.

  • CISA warned that hackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-28318, a recently patched high-severity SolarWinds Serv-U denial-of-service vulnerability, to crash servers.
  • SolarWinds said CVE-2026-28318 is caused by uncontrolled resource consumption and can be triggered by specially crafted unauthenticated POST requests using Content-Encoding: deflate.
  • SolarWinds released Serv-U 15.5.4 Hotfix 1 to fix the flaw and advised administrators who cannot patch immediately to limit access to known addresses and block POST requests containing content-encoding.
  • CISA added CVE-2026-28318 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog and ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to patch affected servers by June 19 under BOD 22-01.
  • Shodan tracks more than 12,000 SolarWinds Serv-U servers exposed online, while Shadowserver tracks just over 3,100, with no information on how many have been patched.

Chinese APT deploys new malware to keep access to hacked networks — Must Read

Chinese espionage group UNC5221/VerdantBamboo maintained long-term access to victim networks and an MSP using Brickstorm plus newly documented Plenet and AgentPSD backdoors. The campaign shows the group’s focus on persistence, stolen credentials, and devices that often lack EDR coverage, such as firewalls, NAS systems, and appliances.

  • Chinese espionage group UNC5221, also tracked as VerdantBamboo, accessed Microsoft 365 environments using the Brickstorm backdoor and newly documented malware named Plenet and AgentPSD.
  • Volexity found that the threat actor had been present in the victim network for at least 18 months before detection and had also compromised the victim organization’s managed services provider.
  • After remediation, VerdantBamboo breached the organization again using stolen credentials to enable and configure SSL VPN access on the victim’s firewall and then deployed custom malware to a Synology NAS device.
  • Plenet is a cross-platform .NET-based backdoor that provides interactive shell access, remote command execution, file manipulation, and command-and-control server switching.
  • AgentPSD is a Python-based reverse shell utility that Volexity believes was used as a fallback persistence mechanism if other malware was no longer accessible.

IronWorm and New Miasma Worm Variant Hit npm in Supply Chain Attacks — Must Read

Multiple npm supply chain attacks are spreading Rust-based IronWorm and a new Miasma variant through trojanized packages, stealing developer and CI/CD secrets and using compromised credentials to publish more poisoned packages. The campaigns abuse GitHub, npm Trusted Publishing, GitHub Actions, and even AI coding assistant configurations to propagate and persist.

  • Multiple npm supply chain attacks used malicious and poisoned versions of more than 50 legitimate packages to distribute the Rust-based IronWorm information stealer and a self-spreading worm.
  • JFrog said IronWorm steals secrets from developer machines, uses an eBPF kernel rootkit, communicates with its operator over Tor, and propagates by publishing trojanized npm packages with stolen credentials.
  • IronWorm activity was traced to a compromised npm account named asteroiddao, which published package versions containing a Rust ELF binary executed through a preinstall hook.
  • A distinct campaign compromised 57 npm packages across more than 286 malicious versions to distribute a new Miasma worm variant that exfiltrated stolen data to a GitHub account named liuende501.
  • StepSecurity said the new Miasma variant uses a “Phantom Gyp” technique, abusing a small binding.gyp file to trigger code execution during npm install instead of relying on preinstall or postinstall lifecycle scripts.

AWS Login Phishing Attack: How Attackers Exploit New Authentication — Should Read

Researchers show that AWS’s new aws login --remote flow can be abused for phishing by hosting the lure on legitimate amazonaws.com infrastructure and relaying the victim’s real AWS authentication to an attacker-controlled CLI session. Organizations should block the required OAuth sign-in actions with SCPs where possible and monitor CloudTrail for cross-device login events with mismatched IPs or user agents.

  • AWS introduced the aws login command with a --remote option that generates a cross-device authentication URL for browser-based sign-in and returns credentials to the AWS CLI.
  • The article describes a phishing method in which attackers run aws login --remote on their own infrastructure, lure victims to authenticate through legitimate AWS sign-in pages, and use the verification code to complete the attacker’s CLI login.
  • The researchers built a proof-of-concept phishing setup using AWS services including S3, DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway, EC2, and SSM, causing the phishing infrastructure to reside on amazonaws.com domains.
  • The article recommends preventing the demonstrated flow by using Service Control Policies to deny signin:AuthorizeOAuth2Access and signin:CreateOAuth2Token where cross-device or CLI-based sign-in is not needed.
  • The article states that detection should focus on CloudTrail events with requestParameters.client_id set to arn:aws:signin:::devtools/cross-device and mismatches in source IP address, user agent, timing, or geography between AuthorizeOAuth2Access and CreateOAuth2Token events.

Suspicious Polyfill login prompts pop up on Toshiba, Muji websites — Should Read

Toshiba, Muji, and other Japanese websites displayed suspicious browser login prompts caused by lingering polyfill.io code after the domain became active again and returned HTTP 401 authentication requests. Users who entered credentials are advised to change their passwords, though there is no confirmed evidence of site compromise or credential theft.

  • Toshiba and Muji warned visitors that suspicious sign-in screens on their websites could collect credentials and advised users who entered login data to change their passwords.
  • The login pop-ups were generated by the external service hosted at polyfill.io, which had introduced malicious code in scripts delivered by its CDN in 2024.
  • Both Toshiba and Muji said they had solved the issue and suspended the polyfill.io service on their websites.
  • Japanese media reported that Zojirushi, FiNC Technologies, Ishiyaku Publishers, and Hobonichi were also impacted by the same issue.
  • Security researcher Pasquale Pillitteri reported that in late May 2026 polyfill.io became active again and started responding with HTTP 401 authentication requests, causing browsers to display login prompts.

A New AI Executive Order: What Federal Agencies Need to Know — Should Read

A new U.S. Executive Order directs federal agencies to accelerate AI adoption while strengthening cyber defenses, including AI-enabled security tools, vulnerability coordination, and testing of advanced frontier models. Netskope frames the order as a call for agencies to improve visibility, data protection, and zero trust controls across AI use.

  • The June 2, 2026 Executive Order on AI directs federal agencies to accelerate AI adoption while strengthening cybersecurity and national security protections.
  • The order calls for the Secretary of Homeland Security, through CISA and in consultation with OMB, to release Binding Operational Directives within 30 days to strengthen protections across federal environments.
  • The order requires the creation of an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse to coordinate vulnerability scanning, validation, remediation prioritization, and patch distribution with AI industry and critical infrastructure operators.
  • The order directs Treasury, NSA, CISA, and others to develop a classified benchmarking process within 60 days to assess advanced cyber capabilities of AI models and identify thresholds for covered frontier models.
  • Netskope states that it can help federal agencies secure AI usage by providing visibility, data protection, AI-specific threat detection, zero trust enforcement, and red teaming capabilities.

Correlation vs. Behavior-Based Threat Detection: Why Alerts Get Ignored — Optional

Correlation-based alerts can catch known attack sequences, but they are brittle and often low-value when attackers vary tactics or use legitimate access. Security teams get better signal by layering behavioral detection with correlation and focusing on alerts that speed investigation.

  • Correlation-based detections identify predefined sequences of security events within a specific timeframe, but they depend on complete telemetry and can fail when attackers alter timing, ordering, or methodology.
  • Behavior-based detections compare activity against historical baselines to identify deviations, making them more resilient to credential misuse, living-off-the-land techniques, supply chain abuse, and minor attacker adaptations.
  • In cloud, SaaS, and identity-driven environments, attackers increasingly operate within valid permission boundaries, which makes contextual deviations more useful than static rule matches for identifying misuse.
  • The article argues that detection quality should be measured by investigative value, meaning how much an alert helps responders understand what happened and make containment decisions faster.
  • Mature security programs combine correlation-based detection, behavioral analysis, AI enhancement, and disciplined investigation rather than relying on any single detection method.

How a Dangling DNS Entry Can Lead to a Subdomain Takeover — Optional

Dangling DNS records can let attackers claim abandoned third-party resources and take over trusted subdomains for phishing, malware, or other abuse. Censys is promoting ASM detections that continuously identify dangling CNAME, NS, and third-party service risks so teams can remediate them before exploitation.

  • Dangling DNS entries are stale DNS records that still point to resources that no longer exist or are no longer controlled by the organization.
  • Attackers can exploit dangling DNS records by claiming the referenced resource and gaining control of the affected subdomain.
  • Subdomain takeover can enable phishing pages, malware delivery, fake login portals, malicious redirects, defacement content, and credential harvesting infrastructure under a trusted domain.
  • Dangling DNS is common in large organizations because DNS records accumulate across teams, cloud accounts, and infrastructure providers, and removal is often not part of resource offboarding.
  • Censys ASM detects dangling DNS risks across CNAMEs, NS records, and third-party services and provides associated domain names, impacted DNS records, and evidence for each detection.

Health Care and Social Assistance Threat Landscape Report — Optional

ReliaQuest’s Q1 2026 report summarizes the main cyber threats facing the health care and social assistance sector, including ransomware and data-leak activity, vulnerability exploitation, attacker targeting, common techniques, data exposure risks, and targeted countries.

  • The Health Care and Social Assistance Threat Landscape Report was published on June 5, 2026.
  • The report covers the period from January 1 to March 31, 2026.
  • The report was produced by ReliaQuest and focuses on threats affecting the health care and social assistance sector.
  • The report provides insights into ransomware and data-leak sites, vulnerability exploitation, attacker target insights, top techniques, data risk protection, and countries targeted.
  • ReliaQuest is identified as a Champion with Health-ISAC Community Services.

Why AI-Powered Cloud Detection & Response is Critical for Modern SOCs — Optional

The article argues that modern cloud attacks move across identity, SaaS, cloud infrastructure, and workloads in one chain, which legacy SIEM, EDR, and network tools often miss. It promotes AI-powered Cloud Detection and Response as a way to correlate those signals and trigger faster, cloud-native response.

  • The article says modern cloud attacks often move across identity systems, SaaS platforms, cloud infrastructure, workloads, and data services as one continuous attack path.
  • The article argues that legacy SIEM, EDR, and network security tools can create blind spots because they view cloud activity through separate logs, endpoints, or network traffic rather than a full cross-platform attack chain.
  • AI-powered Cloud Detection and Response is described as correlating suspicious activity across platforms such as Okta, Salesforce, AWS, Kubernetes, and S3 to identify connected attacks.
  • The article states that AI-powered CDR can automate cloud-native response actions such as revoking credentials, suspending sessions, isolating Kubernetes clusters, quarantining workloads, and suspending compute instances.
  • The article presents AI-powered CDR as a way to compensate for cloud security skills shortages by translating complex cloud logs, adding attack context, and providing guided response steps.
entry.summary: This coverage window was dominated by reports of trusted infrastructure abuse across web dependencies, developer ecosystems, cloud identity flows, and SaaS platforms. Several items describe attackers using legitimate services, valid credentials, or misconfigurations to create convincing phishing...

entry.title: Asin Android Spyware, Exposed Tank Gauges, and AI Browser Identity Risks
entry.content: This digest highlights a mix of enterprise identity risk, critical infrastructure exposure, mobile surveillance activity, and dark web enforcement developments. Across the reporting, a common theme is the expansion of attack surfaces into browsers, SaaS-connected AI agents, internet-exposed operational systems, and mobile apps used by targeted communities. Mitiga framed emerging AI PCs as a new enterprise identity risk, arguing that locally running agents with SaaS integrations can hold live credentials and act across business applications. The article focused on Nvidia and Microsoft’s RTX Spark ecosystem, which Mitiga says will support a 120-billion-parameter AI agent on new Windows PCs from major vendors including Dell, HP, ASUS, Lenovo, Microsoft Surface, and MSI. The risk described is not simply local endpoint compromise, but inherited authority across connected applications. Mitiga said a compromised AI PC could allow an attacker to leverage the agent’s permissions across services such as Salesforce, SharePoint, and Workday, while noting that Nvidia’s OpenShell can define agent boundaries but does not replace detection, correlation, audit trails, or incident response capabilities. A separate browser-focused analysis tied the 2026 Verizon DBIR to the growth of shadow AI and credential abuse. It reported that shadow AI became the third most common non-malicious insider action in DLP datasets, representing a fourfold increase year over year, while 39% of breaches involved credential abuse. Keep Aware telemetry cited in the article found widespread personal-account use of AI services on corporate devices and described browser-based credential theft as its leading observed browser attack category. U.S. government agencies and partners issued a joint advisory on internet-exposed automatic tank gauge systems used in fuel and critical infrastructure environments. Shadowserver reported 1,061 exposed automatic tank gauge system IPs on June 5, 2026, with 909 located in the United States. The advisory described active exploitation of weaknesses including hardcoded credentials, authentication bypasses, SQL injection, operating system command execution flaws, and privilege escalation vulnerabilities. According to the reporting, attackers have used these issues to modify compromised systems through command execution, underscoring continued exposure of operational technology components to internet-based access. ESET reported a new Android spyware family codenamed Asin targeting Arabic-speaking users through fake utility, news, PDF reader, and war map applications. The activity was first detected in early 2025 and used websites imitating government news, document tools, and conflict-related information sources. Identified distribution infrastructure included govlens.net, pdf-reader.help, live-war-map.com, c-pdf.net, and syriadefensemap.com. ESET also observed promotion through dedicated Facebook and Telegram accounts, including GovLens on Facebook and liveuamapar on Telegram, and assessed that the lures suggest Arabic-speaking journalists or OSINT practitioners may have been among the intended targets. The cluster remains unattributed. U.S. authorities announced that Darren Hughes of San Jose, California, received a sentence of more than 26 years in federal prison for trafficking fentanyl and methamphetamine through Nemesis Market. Hughes operated a store on the market, offered free methamphetamine samples, and later sold methamphetamine and fentanyl pills to an undercover law enforcement agent in exchange for cryptocurrency. The case followed Hughes’ June 28, 2023 arrest by the Redwood City Police Department after another arranged sale with undercover agents. Detectives found approximately 672 grams of methamphetamine and a loaded 9mm ghost gun with no serial number in his vehicle. Nemesis Market itself was taken down by German and American authorities in March 2024 after growing to more than 150,000 user accounts and 1,100 seller accounts.
entry.summary: This digest highlights a mix of enterprise identity risk, critical infrastructure exposure, mobile surveillance activity, and dark web enforcement developments. Across the reporting, a common theme is the expansion of attack surfaces into browsers, SaaS-connected AI agents, internet-exposed...

entry.title: OP-512 IIS Web Shells, protobuf.js Proto6 Flaws, and Enterprise AI Agent Failures
entry.content: This coverage window is dominated by risks emerging from enterprise AI adoption, exposed internet-facing infrastructure, and identity-centric fraud. Reporting also highlighted European policy moves around technology sovereignty and software assurance, alongside concrete vulnerability research affecting protobuf.js and protobufjs-cli. Cyera reported that its analysis of 7,246 publicly reported AI incident records from September 2023 through May 2026 identified 344 verified enterprise-relevant cases of agent-inflicted damage. Of those, 188 involved autonomous AI systems causing direct organizational harm without an external attacker, with incidents grouped into poor access control and privilege escalation, data and secrets exposure, and real-world damage such as financial loss, code destruction, service disruption, and silent integrity failure. The research said incident volume rose sharply beginning in December 2025, coinciding with broader enterprise adoption of AI coding agents and autonomous development tools including Claude Code, Cursor agent mode, Devin, and OpenClaw. Health-ISAC separately warned that agentic AI in healthcare can intensify risks around over-permissioned accounts, weak governance, and credential misuse as AI-enabled workflows take on delegated operational decisions. In security operations, the SOC-CMM 2026 Maturity Report found that only about 10% of surveyed SOCs reported excellent value from AI, while roughly 71% reported only some value or no value. The reporting attributed weaker outcomes to first-wave AI features embedded in isolated SIEM, EDR, SOAR, and ticketing workflows, while describing stronger-performing SOCs as those applying AI across threat intelligence, hunting, detection engineering, investigation, and remediation with shared context and governance. ReliaQuest reported a previously unreported threat cluster tracked as OP-512 targeting Microsoft IIS servers with a custom web shell framework. The activity was assessed with moderate to high confidence as China-linked and espionage-focused, and the observed target was a legacy IIS server running Windows Server 2016 with end-of-life .NET Framework 4.0. The OP-512 framework reportedly consists of three web shells enabling remote access, file management, authenticated command execution through two access paths, and automated compromise reporting. The web shells used timestomping by calculating the median last-modified timestamp of nearby files and folders, then modifying their own timestamps to blend into the file system; the actor also attempted privilege escalation to SYSTEM using the Potato Suite. Cyera also disclosed six vulnerabilities in protobuf.js under the name Proto6, affecting scenarios where schema-derived data is poisoned or crafted. The findings included a runtime remote code execution chain requiring a separate prototype pollution primitive, a protobufjs-cli static generation flaw that can emit crafted schema names into generated JavaScript that executes on import, and recursion-based denial-of-service cases reproduced against Baileys WhatsApp bots and a Google Cloud Functions Pub/Sub decoding pattern; fixes were released in protobuf.js 7.5.6 and 8.0.2, and protobufjs-cli 1.2.1 and 2.0.2. Cyble reported that executive impersonation has become a highly targeted and financially damaging attack vector for organizations in the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar in 2026. Gulf executives were described as attractive targets because of their roles in large international transactions, visibility on LinkedIn, and frequent connections to government-linked, energy, or sovereign wealth entities. The reported methods include LinkedIn impersonation, WhatsApp CEO fraud, lookalike domains, and AI-generated voice or video deepfake fraud. The article also noted that Saudi Arabia’s SAMA Cybersecurity Framework requires financial-sector organizations to address social engineering and targeted attacks against key personnel, including monitoring unauthorized executive identity use and maintaining response procedures. IRONSCALES described a funding-fraud email sent from a typosquat domain with one extra letter compared with a legitimate commercial lender. The domain was configured as a Salesforce Marketing Cloud sending identity, allowing the message to pass SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and Microsoft CompAuth checks, while Pardot tracking links redirected to the legitimate lender’s site and the reply-to address pointed back to the typosquat domain; Microsoft still scored it as high-confidence spam with SCL=9 and SFTY:9.25. The European Commission proposed a technology sovereignty package covering Chips Act 2.0, the Cloud and AI Development Act, an Open Source Strategy, and a roadmap for digitalizing the energy system. The Commission said the EU depends on foreign countries for more than 80% of key digital products, services, infrastructure, and intellectual property. The Open Source Strategy would fund long-term maintenance and security for Europe’s critical open-source infrastructure and steer public administrations toward open-source tools through procurement guidance. The Cloud and AI Development Act defines four assurance levels for public bodies, with higher tiers requiring EU ownership, EU control, and protection from third-country interference, while Chips Act 2.0 would accelerate approval timelines for new fabrication plants and extend state aid for first-of-a-kind facilities not already present in the EU.
entry.summary: This coverage window is dominated by risks emerging from enterprise AI adoption, exposed internet-facing infrastructure, and identity-centric fraud. Reporting also highlighted European policy moves around technology sovereignty and software assurance, alongside concrete vulnerability research...

entry.title: CVE-2026-3300 WordPress RCE, Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day, and FIFA 2026 Fraud Surge
entry.content: This coverage window is dominated by active exploitation of edge and web-facing platforms, alongside large-scale fraud activity tied to the FIFA World Cup 2026. The most technically significant items are an unauthenticated WordPress plugin RCE affecting Everest Forms Pro and an unpatched Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager zero-day used for root privilege escalation. Threat actors are actively exploiting CVE-2026-3300, a critical remote code execution vulnerability in the Everest Forms Pro WordPress plugin affecting versions up to and including 1.9.12. Wordfence attributed the flaw to unsafe handling in the Calculation Addon's processfilter() function, where user-submitted form values are concatenated into PHP code without proper escaping before being passed to eval(). Successful exploitation allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary PHP code on vulnerable WordPress sites. Reported post-exploitation outcomes include creation of rogue administrator accounts, deployment of web shells, and persistence on compromised servers. Everest Forms Pro 1.9.13, released on March 18, 2026, contains the fix for the vulnerability. Cisco separately warned that CVE-2026-20245, a high-severity zero-day in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, is being actively exploited and remains unpatched. The flaw affects all deployment models, including on-premises, Cisco-managed cloud, Cloud-Pro, and FedRAMP environments, and allows local attackers with netadmin privileges to upload crafted files and execute arbitrary commands as root. Cisco linked the issue to insufficient validation of user-supplied input and published indicators involving activity in /var/log/scripts.log related to attempts to upload tenant configuration data to vSmart controllers. Researchers and law enforcement are reporting an early wave of FIFA World Cup 2026-themed cybercrime, including fake FIFA domains, cloned login portals, fraudulent ticket offers, counterfeit merchandise stores, bogus streaming sites, and fake betting platforms. Group-IB tracked more than 4,300 fraudulent FIFA-related domains registered since August 2025 and linked over 300 cloned FIFA sites to a Chinese-speaking operation it tracks as GHOST STADIUM. GHOST STADIUM phishing pages closely imitate FIFA’s legitimate login flow and prompt victims to reset passwords, enabling attackers to seize accounts and potentially monetize tickets associated with them. The campaign illustrates how major global sporting events continue to drive early credential-harvesting infrastructure long before the event itself, especially where account access, ticket resale, and fan payment activity intersect. Mobile malware is also appearing in the FIFA-themed threat ecosystem. ThreatFabric and Kaspersky reported unofficial Android streaming apps impersonating services such as RojaDirecta and delivering banking trojans including Massiv and Perseus, both of which can abuse Android accessibility features to steal credentials and intercept verification codes. Fortinet also found hundreds of thousands of user logins and more than 4,600 FIFA web addresses in data gathered by credential-stealing malware families including Vidar, LummaC2, and RedLine. Sansec reported separate skimmer campaigns that abuse trusted services including Stripe, Google Tag Manager, and Google Firestore for command-and-control and data exfiltration. This activity reflects an ongoing pattern in which attackers embed malicious commerce infrastructure within services that defenders and customers are less likely to distrust by default. The skimmer reporting sits adjacent to the Everest Forms Pro exploitation because both affect the broader web commerce and CMS ecosystem, where attackers can convert site compromise into credential theft, payment data theft, or persistent access. Together, these reports show continued pressure on web-facing applications, plugins, and third-party service integrations that sit directly in user transaction flows.
entry.summary: This coverage window is dominated by active exploitation of edge and web-facing platforms, alongside large-scale fraud activity tied to the FIFA World Cup 2026. The most technically significant items are an unauthenticated WordPress plugin RCE affecting Everest Forms Pro and an unpatched Cisco...

entry.title: PCPJack Builds Covert SMTP Relay Network Across AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure
entry.content: Reporting in this coverage window centers on PCPJack, a cloud-focused operation that hijacked servers across AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure to build a covert SMTP relay network. The activity combines exposed command-and-control infrastructure, Linux persistence, proxy orchestration, and automated validation of email relay capability, indicating a structured effort to turn compromised cloud hosts into abuse-ready infrastructure. PCPJack reportedly hijacked 230 cloud servers associated with AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure for use as a covert SMTP email relay network. Hunt.io identified operational artifacts in two unauthenticated open directories hosted on a command-and-control server at 213.136.80[.]73, including source code, compiled binaries, deployment logs, scanners, exploitation tooling, and a live Sliver configuration. The exposed materials provide insight into the campaign’s tradecraft and infrastructure management. Victim-side tooling was deployed as a hidden dot-prefixed binary and persisted at /var/tmp/.xs, while deployment scripts checked for Linux Sliver beacons before assigning each active beacon a deterministic SOCKS5 proxy port. Early-stage logic also skipped hosts that could not reach smtp.gmail[.]com:587, suggesting the operators prioritized compromised systems capable of outbound SMTP submission or relay testing. The operation appears designed to maintain and curate a working pool of cloud-based proxy relays rather than simply compromise hosts opportunistically. Verified proxy lists were enriched with exit IP address, country, and ASN metadata, then synchronized every five minutes via SCP to a downstream server at 38.242.204[.]245. This enrichment and frequent syncing indicate an operational pipeline for tracking relay quality, geographic distribution, and provider diversity. The abuse of cloud infrastructure across multiple major providers is notable because it allows threat actors to blend relay activity into reputable hosting environments. The use of Sliver, SOCKS5 proxy assignment, automated reachability checks, and downstream relay inventory management points to a campaign focused on scalable infrastructure enablement, likely supporting spam, phishing, or other email-based abuse where sender reputation, IP diversity, and relay availability are operationally valuable.
entry.summary: Reporting in this coverage window centers on PCPJack, a cloud-focused operation that hijacked servers across AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure to build a covert SMTP relay network. The activity combines exposed command-and-control infrastructure, Linux persistence, proxy orchestration, and...

entry.title: CVE-2026-20245, Serv-U CVE-2026-28318, ShinyHunters Salesforce Abuse, and npm Worms
entry.content: The 24-hour coverage window was dominated by active exploitation, cloud and SaaS identity abuse, software supply chain compromises, and growing concern over AI-enabled enterprise risk. Several reports also highlighted attacks against exposed infrastructure, including Cisco SD-WAN, SolarWinds Serv-U, WordPress plugins, fuel-sector tank gauges, and legacy IIS servers. A recurring theme was attacker use of trusted platforms and legitimate workflows: AWS sign-in flows, Salesforce Experience Cloud sites, npm package installs, Pardot tracking links, and public cloud infrastructure all appeared in reported attack paths. The result is a threat landscape where credential theft, misconfiguration, and supply chain trust relationships remain central to intrusion and fraud activity. Cisco disclosed active exploitation of CVE-2026-20245, an unpatched Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager zero-day that enables root privilege escalation by local attackers with low and netadmin privileges. Cisco said the issue affects all deployment types and shared indicators involving suspicious entries in /var/log/scripts.log, while patches for this specific flaw were not yet available. CISA added CVE-2026-28318, a SolarWinds Serv-U denial-of-service flaw, to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog after attackers began exploiting crafted unauthenticated POST requests with Content-Encoding: deflate to crash servers. Separately, U.S. agencies warned that more than 900 internet-exposed automatic tank gauge systems in the United States were vulnerable to attacks involving hardcoded credentials, authentication bypass, SQL injection, command execution, and privilege escalation. Wordfence reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-3300 in Everest Forms Pro for WordPress, where unsafe use of eval() in the Calculation Addon can allow unauthenticated remote code execution. ReliaQuest also described OP-512, a China-linked espionage cluster targeting Microsoft IIS servers with a custom three-web-shell framework, timestomping, and attempted privilege escalation using Potato Suite tooling. Mitiga detailed a phishing method abusing the AWS aws login --remote flow, where attackers can initiate authentication from their own infrastructure, send a legitimate AWS sign-in URL to a victim, and use the returned verification code to obtain a valid session. The proof-of-concept used AWS services including S3, DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway, EC2, and SSM, making the lure appear to originate from Amazon-controlled infrastructure. ShinyHunters reportedly abused misconfigured Salesforce Experience Cloud Guest User Profiles through the /s/sfsites/aura endpoint, allegedly affecting hundreds of organizations and exposing CRM data such as customer names, phone numbers, financial information, and business relationships. Related reporting on executive impersonation in the Gulf and a Salesforce Marketing Cloud typosquat funding-fraud email showed how attackers combine trusted SaaS platforms, lookalike domains, and social engineering against high-value personnel. Browser and domain trust risks also featured prominently. Reports covered suspicious polyfill.io-triggered login prompts on Toshiba, Muji, and other Japanese sites, dangling DNS records enabling subdomain takeover, and World Cup 2026 scams using fake FIFA domains, cloned login flows, malicious Android streaming apps, and infostealer-exposed credentials. Multiple reports focused on package ecosystem compromise. JFrog described IronWorm, a Rust-based npm infostealer that hides behind an eBPF kernel rootkit, communicates over Tor, steals developer secrets, and propagates by publishing trojanized npm packages from compromised accounts. Endor Labs and StepSecurity separately reported a new Miasma worm variant spread through 57 npm packages and more than 286 malicious versions using a Phantom Gyp technique to trigger install-time code execution. Mitiga described TeamPCP’s abuse of a misconfigured GitHub Actions pullrequesttarget workflow in Aqua Security’s Trivy repository to expose a bot token, then force-push release tags in aquasecurity/trivy-action to malicious commits. The broader campaign reportedly moved through GitHub Actions, Docker Hub, npm, Open VSX, PyPI, and cloud environments using stolen credentials as lateral movement tools. Cyera disclosed six protobuf.js vulnerabilities under the Proto6 research, including remote code execution and denial-of-service scenarios when schema-derived data is poisoned or crafted. In the defensive ecosystem, RubyGems and Bundler introduced an opt-in cooldown feature to delay installation of newly published gem versions, reflecting continued concern over rapid automated consumption of malicious package releases. Volexity reported that Chinese espionage group UNC5221, also known as VerdantBamboo, maintained access to a victim environment for at least 18 months using Brickstorm and newly documented malware named Plenet and AgentPSD. The actor also compromised the victim’s managed services provider and later re-entered using stolen credentials to configure SSL VPN access and deploy malware to a Synology NAS device. Hunt.io reported PCPJack activity involving hijacked AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure servers used to build a covert SMTP relay network. Investigators found tooling, binaries, deployment logs, scanners, exploitation code, and a live Sliver configuration in exposed command-and-control directories, with victim-side persistence at /var/tmp/.xs. ESET described Asin, an Android spyware family targeting Arabic-speaking users through fake news, PDF reader, war map, and government-themed sites promoted through Facebook and Telegram. The cluster remains unattributed, but the lures suggest possible targeting of Arabic-speaking journalists or OSINT practitioners. Several articles examined AI’s operational impact on security teams and enterprise risk. Cyera analyzed more than 7,000 public AI incident records and identified 344 enterprise-relevant agent-inflicted damage cases, including incidents involving access control failures, data and secrets exposure, deletion or code destruction, service disruption, and silent integrity failure. Mitiga and Health-ISAC separately framed agentic AI and AI PCs as emerging identity and governance risks, particularly where agents hold credentials or act across SaaS platforms such as CRM, email, storage, and productivity tools. The SOC-CMM 2026 Maturity Report found that only about 10% of SOCs reported excellent value from AI, while other coverage described AI-powered cloud detection and response as an effort to correlate activity across identity, SaaS, workloads, and cloud platforms. Policy and sector reporting also advanced. Netskope summarized a June 2, 2026 Executive Order directing U.S. federal agencies to accelerate AI adoption while strengthening cyber defenses, and Health-ISAC published its Q1 2026 Health Care and Social Assistance Threat Landscape Report covering ransomware, vulnerability exploitation, attacker targeting, techniques, and data risk.
entry.summary: The 24-hour coverage window was dominated by active exploitation, cloud and SaaS identity abuse, software supply chain compromises, and growing concern over AI-enabled enterprise risk. Several reports also highlighted attacks against exposed infrastructure, including Cisco SD-WAN, SolarWinds...

entry.title: June 2026 AI Executive Order Sets Federal Cyber Defense Requirements
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by policy-driven cyber defense developments rather than newly reported intrusion activity. The covered reporting focuses on the June 2026 AI Executive Order and its implications for U.S. federal civilian agencies, particularly around AI-enabled cyber defense, vulnerability management, and interagency coordination. A Tenable analysis summarizes requirements from the June 2, 2026 Executive Order directing federal agencies to begin hardening information systems with AI-enabled cyber defenses within 30 days. The order assigns CISA a central role in issuing Binding Operational Directives or other guidance for civilian federal agencies, with an emphasis on prioritizing cyber defense and expanding agency access to AI-enabled defensive tooling. The order also directs the Department of the Treasury, working with NSA and CISA, to establish an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse. According to the article, the clearinghouse is intended to coordinate vulnerability scanning, validation, remediation prioritization, and patch distribution across participating entities. This reflects a federal push to operationalize AI in vulnerability and exposure management workflows, rather than limiting AI policy to research or governance functions. Within 60 days, Treasury, NSA, and CISA are also required to develop a classified benchmarking process for assessing advanced cyber capabilities of frontier AI models. The process is described as relying on voluntary collaboration with AI developers and is intended to evaluate cyber-relevant model capabilities in a controlled classified setting. Tenable frames the Executive Order as aligned with exposure management practices, stating that its platform can support asset and vulnerability discovery, remediation prioritization using Vulnerability Priority Rating and Known Exploited Vulnerabilities data, and visibility into AI-related exposures. The company also highlights Tenable Hexa AI as a mechanism for automating remediation workflows. The article is vendor-authored, but it provides a concise view of how federal AI cybersecurity mandates are being interpreted by the exposure management market.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by policy-driven cyber defense developments rather than newly reported intrusion activity. The covered reporting focuses on the June 2026 AI Executive Order and its implications for U.S. federal civilian agencies, particularly around AI-enabled cyber defense,...
